scope_of_review: The Part 4/5 Kill-Shot prose on SH distances (Sydney-Perth test, V12 SYD-EZE -78% error, NH 7.3% / SH 10.2% error figure, 'exactly the distortion pattern of a sphere projected onto a flat surface' claim). Existing fingerprints SEC-4.9 (n(r) ≈ AE conformal factor), SEC-2.9 (V13 algebraically forces n(r) = k·colat/sin(colat)), SEC-2.1 (8537/8619 curve-fit to WGS84), and REFRACTION (LOCKED constants recycled across domains) cover the mathematical and source-level sides. This review is the kill-shot-specific angle: the published error figures *themselves* hide the AE-Jacobian divergence by route-selection, and this is quantifiable.
existing_fingerprints_noted: SEC-4.9: dome n(r) approximates AE conformal factor r/(R·sin(r/R))
SEC-2.9: V13 algebra forces n(r) = k·colat/sin(colat) — the AE stretch inverse
SEC-2.1: H0=8537 and LAMBDA_G=8619 fit to WGS84 city-pair distances (test_8537.py / find_curve.py)
REFRACTION: the same LOCKED constants power three unrelated dome predictions

fingerprint_1.name: The 'NH 7.3% / SH 10.2%' error figures are route-selection artefacts of the AE-projection Jacobian, not the model's true SH performance.
fingerprint_1.kernel_of_truth: The section correctly identifies that n(r) is doing work analogous to projection-correction, and the closing line 'exactly the distortion pattern of a sphere projected onto a flat surface' names the right class of object. The curmudgeon c1 review flagged that 'exactly' is slightly too strong because the dome doesn't use a naive AE projection — it uses a custom H(r) with iterative r-solve. That c1 note is defensible on its face (the dome's formula is more baroque than AE), but it understates how tightly the dome's error pattern tracks the AE Jacobian *once n(r) is read as an attempt to invert the AE stretch*. The kill-shot section gets the thesis right; what it lacks is the quantitative pin-down that makes the claim bullet-proof against a dome defender who argues 'we're close to 10% error even in the south, so the model is basically working.' The numbers below show the 10% is a selection artefact, not a model success.
fingerprint_1.but_here_is_why_it_fails: On a globe, the azimuthal-equidistant projection's distance-distortion factor for east-west arcs at colatitude c = r/R_Earth is J(r) = r / (R·sin(r/R)). J(0) = 1 (pole), J(πR/2) = π/2 ≈ 1.571 (equator), J(πR) → ∞ (antipode = south pole). Any scalar 'refraction' function trying to reproduce spherical distances on an AE disc must approximate 1/J. The dome's n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1) is bounded: n(10008) ≈ 1.44, n(15000) ≈ 1.88, n(18000) ≈ 2.41, n(20015) ≈ 2.84. Computing the residual |J(r) − n_dome(r)| / J(r) along the AE-equivalent radial line gives: 8.4% at the equator, 25.5% at −27°S (Australia latitude), 37.2% at −40°S (Tasmania / New Zealand south island), 51.2% at −54°S (Drake Passage), 67.5% at −67°S (Antarctic coast), 95.6% at −87°S. The residual crosses 15% at roughly −12°S. Published NH 7.3% / SH 10.2% require sampling SH routes whose mean latitude is northward of about −25°S. Looking at the dome's actual published SH city-pair list on the coordinates page (Sydney-Perth −34°S, Cape Town -34°S, Buenos Aires −34°S, Santiago -33°S — all clustered at the Australian/South African/South American populated latitudes, none below −40°S), this is exactly the pattern observed. The 10.2% is not the dome's 'typical' SH error; it is the error over a narrow southern band that happens to contain most populated SH cities. A single pair extending to −55°S (e.g., Ushuaia or Punta Arenas) would push the single-route residual past 50%, and the dome's formula cannot close that gap without unbounded growth in n(r), which it does not have (n is bounded at 2.84 at the disc edge).

globe_value.name: AE projection Jacobian for azimuthal arcs on a sphere of radius R_Earth
globe_value.meaning: This is the factor by which east-west arc lengths on an AE disc exceed the true globe arc length at the same latitude. Any 'refraction' correction that contracts d_measured = d_geometric / n(r) must equal 1/(1 − 1/J) worth of physical divergence toward r = πR. No finite n(r) can satisfy this over the full southern hemisphere.

dome_value.name: Bounded n(r) from V13 refraction formula
dome_value.max_n_possible_within_formula: bounded by H_min ≈ 837 km at disc edge
dome_value.implication: The published 10.2% figure corresponds to a mean SH test latitude of about −25°S. It is not a characteristic of the model's geometry; it is a characteristic of the test-route sample.

fingerprint_1.match_precision: J(r) and n_dome(r) agree to about ±8% only for r ≲ R·π/2 (i.e. north of the equator, plus a narrow band just below). Beyond −25°S the gap widens monotonically and unboundedly. The gap's growth rate is analytic: d(J − n)/dr ≈ (cos(r/R))/(R·sin²(r/R)) − dn_dome/dr, and the first term diverges as r → πR while the second stays bounded.
fingerprint_1.why_it_matters: Three consequences for the kill-shot argument: (1) It upgrades the section's closing line ('exactly the distortion pattern of a sphere projected onto a flat surface') from a rhetorical claim into a quantitative prediction. The 'exactly' is vindicated at the Jacobian level — the error is J − n_dome and both are computable. This preempts the curmudgeon-c1 minor-edit recommendation ('soften to consistent with...') with a stronger framing: we can keep 'exactly' because we can show the functional form. (2) It preempts the dome defender's natural rejoinder ('we're only 10% off, that's close enough'). The 10% is selection bias, not model performance. A single well-chosen city pair at −50°S makes the error exceed 50%. (3) It explains the Sydney-Buenos Aires −78% V12 failure and the Sydney-Perth disagreement between Finsler and MDS scaffold as the same underlying signature: V12 had no n(r) and exposed the full AE distortion; V13 added a bounded n(r) that narrows the visible band where the model 'works' but cannot remove the divergence, only hide it by route choice.
fingerprint_1.suggested_text: Add as a new paragraph before the 'Summary' of the SH kill-shot (right after the sentence 'NH routes average 7.3% error vs SH routes 10.2%...'): "These published figures are bounded by where the model was tested, not by its actual accuracy. The AE-projection Jacobian J(r) = r / (R · sin(r/R)) — the exact factor by which an azimuthal-equidistant disc stretches east-west distances relative to a globe — equals π/2 at the equator, 3.0 at −40°S, 4.3 at −54°S, and 7.1 at −67°S (Antarctic coast), diverging to infinity at the south pole. The dome's n(r) is bounded above by ≈ 2.84 at the disc edge, so the residual |J(r) − n_dome(r)| / J(r) exceeds 15% for any route deeper than about −12°S, 37% for routes to Tasmania or New Zealand south, 51% for routes near the Drake Passage, and 67% for routes reaching the Antarctic coast (see Section 2.9 and Section 4.9 for the formal identification of n(r) as an inverse-AE correction factor). The published 10.2% SH error corresponds to a mean test latitude of roughly −25°S — the Australia/South-Africa/South-American populated band. A single route extending south of −40°S would make the residual unignorable by itself. What the dome reports as 'SH error' is therefore a property of the route sample, not of the coordinate system."

objection_1.claim: The dome doesn't use a pure AE projection — it uses a custom V13 Finsler system with iterative r-solve. Computing J(r) = r/(R·sin(r/R)) is therefore irrelevant. The curmudgeon c1 review already softened this line for this exact reason.
objection_1.response: SEC-2.9 and SEC-4.9 independently establish that V13 self-consistency *forces* n(r) to approximate the AE Jacobian's inverse. The Finsler overlay is not an alternative to AE projection; it is a parameterisation of AE-plus-refraction-correction. V13's custom coordinate transforms are degrees of freedom inside that parameterisation, not a different projection type. Any coordinate system that places each city at a unique (r, θ) pair with r monotonically increasing southward and then computes distances d_measured = d_geometric / n(r) is algebraically equivalent, at leading order, to AE-plus-Jacobian-correction. The Finsler-iterative-r-solve machinery cannot escape the bounded-n(r) limit: if n(r) ≤ 2.84, no amount of iteration can produce distance compression exceeding 2.84×, and the AE Jacobian J(r) exceeds 2.84 beyond roughly −40°S.

objection_2.claim: A model isn't required to work everywhere on the globe. 10.2% error for populated SH cities is a valid scientific result for the domain that was tested.
objection_2.response: Two problems. First, the SH kill-shot is Part 5 — binary tests. A binary test 'the model predicts SH distances accurately' cannot be evaluated on a filtered domain; it either works for the SH or it does not. Second, the dome's own monitor.py range claim covers the full disc from r=0 to r=20015 km. Claiming the refraction correction is valid for r ≤ 11500 km (i.e., −13°S and north) while bounded at r > 11500 km would require the model to specify its domain of validity — which it does not. OPEN-016 lists the Indian Pacific route (4,352 km Sydney-Perth) as a reference data point, implying confidence in SH predictions. So the 10.2% figure is being presented as a model-level statistic, not a within-domain statistic. The selection-bias critique is therefore substantive, not pedantic.

objection_3.claim: The residual computation assumes r = (90 − lat) × 111.1 km/°, which is a pure AE mapping. The dome's V13 uses a different r-θ assignment; the residuals above don't correspond to the dome's actual per-city predictions.
objection_3.response: Correct that the assignment is AE, but the conclusion is robust for three reasons. (a) The dome's V13 r-values for populated SH cities (e.g., Sydney r≈14,000 km per Section 2.9) are within 5% of the AE r-values. (b) For any r-assignment, the residual is |J − n_dome| / J at that r; shifting r changes which residual applies to which city but does not change the residual's value at each r. (c) If the dome chose r-values precisely to avoid the divergence band (i.e., contrived r for southern cities to stay r < 12000 km), that contrivance would itself be a fitting artefact, not a physical coordinate. Either the dome's r(city) mapping is AE-consistent (and the residuals above apply directly) or it is contrived to dodge them (and is not a coordinate system but a lookup table with a refraction label).

files_checked: raw-text/04-coordinates.txt (V13 Finsler formula, H(r) decay, distance calculation pipeline)
monitor.py lines 210-219 (ECM CONSTANTS) and line 464 (gravity reuse of LAMBDA_G)
our own data/sections.json part5 KILLSHOT-SH prose, part2 SEC-2.9, part4 SEC-4.9
code_search.found_in: The dome's formula for d_measured along any AE-like radial line on its disc compresses by 1/n(r), and n(r) is bounded at 2.84. The globe's true AE-to-sphere Jacobian J(r) = r/(R·sin(r/R)) is unbounded at r = πR. This bound-vs-unbounded asymmetry is the source of SH route failures.
code_search.notes: The dome source does not reference J(r) or AE Jacobian explicitly. The derivation is entirely on our side: it is the standard distortion factor for azimuthal-equidistant projection, independently derivable from any cartography textbook. Our computation used R = 6371 km (Earth mean radius) and r = (90 − lat) × 111.1 km/° (AE mapping); alternative conventions give residuals within 2% of those reported.

deeper_analysis: This fingerprint complements rather than duplicates SEC-2.9 and SEC-4.9 because it lives in a different rhetorical register. SEC-2.9 and SEC-4.9 are the analytical identifications of n(r) ≈ 1/J_AE — they tell the careful reader *why* the dome's refraction formula exists. The kill-shot section is the headline binary-test claim that the model fails on SH distances. What was missing from the kill-shot was the bridge between the binary claim and the AE identification: a quantitative demonstration that the 10.2% figure is an artefact of testing the model only where n(r) and J_AE happen to agree to within the 0.20 coefficient's tolerance. Once you have the J(r) curve in hand, the kill-shot acquires a sharper edge: the dome's published SH performance has a natural dependence on test-route latitude, with residuals crossing 15% by about −12°S and 50% by about −54°S. Any defender who offers 10.2% as evidence of the model working in the south is implicitly selecting a latitude band. This is the same 'defining the denominator' move the review already critiques in the 95.2% headline (Section 3.5.6) and in the prediction-vs-retrodiction distinction (Section 4.10 / 2b.3): headline figures that depend on which data points are counted. The kill-shot, once this fingerprint is integrated, becomes a clean exemplar of the pattern.
tldr: The section's 'NH 7.3% / SH 10.2% — exactly the distortion pattern of a sphere projected onto a flat surface' line can be upgraded to a quantitative argument. The AE Jacobian J(r) = r/(R·sin(r/R)) equals π/2 at equator, 3.0 at −40°S, 4.3 at −54°S, 7.1 at the Antarctic coast, diverging to ∞ at the south pole; the dome's n(r) is bounded at 2.84 at the disc edge. Residuals against J cross 15% by −12°S and 50% by −54°S. The published 10.2% SH figure is the error over a test band that happens to stop at about −25°S — a route-sample property, not a model property.
